name: Attention Follows Visual Weight
principle: The largest, boldest, most contrast-rich element attracts the eye first.
perceptual_basis: Pre-attentive processing. The brain detects size and contrast before conscious thought.
violation: All elements same size, weight, or color. Reader must decide importance on their own.
diagnosis: Squint at the design. What do you see first? Is that what should be seen first?
fix: Increase size, weight, or contrast of the most important element. Reduce treatment of secondary elements.

name: One Primary Focus Per View
principle: Every screen, page, or section should have one dominant focal point.
perceptual_basis: Figure-ground. The brain needs a clear figure to organize around.
violation: Multiple elements competing for equal attention. The eye bounces without landing.
diagnosis: Count the elements that could be 'the main thing.' If more than one, hierarchy is broken.
fix: Choose one primary element. De-emphasize all others through size, color, or position.

name: Hierarchy Must Be Visible Without Reading
principle: A reader should understand the information structure from layout alone, before reading a word.
perceptual_basis: Pre-attentive processing + Gestalt. Layout communicates structure faster than content.
violation: Structure only visible through reading text. All text blocks look the same.
diagnosis: Convert the design to grayscale and blur it. Can you still tell the hierarchy?
fix: Use size, weight, spacing, and position to create structure that is visible without reading.

name: Proximity Signals Relationship
principle: Items close together are perceived as related. Items far apart are perceived as separate.
violation: Equal spacing between all items regardless of relationship. Reader must figure out groupings.
diagnosis: Draw circles around groups. If the groups are not obvious without the circles, grouping is insufficient.
fix: Reduce spacing between related items. Increase spacing between unrelated items.

name: Similarity Signals Type
principle: Items of the same type should share visual treatment (color, size, style).
violation: Same content type styled differently across the document.
diagnosis: List all elements of the same type. Do they look the same? If not, consistency is broken.
fix: Standardize visual treatment for each content type. One heading style, one link style, one caption style.

name: Boundaries Clarify Groups
principle: When proximity alone is insufficient, use visual boundaries (cards, borders, background color, whitespace).
violation: Dense content with no visual separation between sections.
diagnosis: Remove all whitespace. If the content becomes an undifferentiated wall, boundaries are needed.
fix: Add containers, borders, or background color to separate distinct groups.

name: Spacing Should Follow a Scale
principle: Use a consistent spacing scale (4px, 8px, 16px, 24px, 32px, 48px, 64px). Never use arbitrary values.
perceptual_basis: Consistency and expectation. Predictable spacing reduces decision-making.
violation: Random spacing values (13px, 17px, 22px, 31px).
diagnosis: Measure spacing between similar elements. If values vary without reason, rhythm is broken.
fix: Establish a spacing scale and apply it proportionally.

name: Vertical Rhythm Creates Flow
principle: Consistent vertical spacing between sections creates a reading cadence.
perceptual_basis: Continuity. The eye follows predictable vertical paths.
violation: Spacing varies dramatically between sections.
diagnosis: Measure the vertical space between consecutive sections. If it varies by more than 25%, rhythm is inconsistent.
fix: Apply consistent inter-section spacing. Use the spacing scale.

name: Repetition Builds Trust
principle: Repeated visual patterns create familiarity and reduce learning cost.
violation: Every section introduces a new visual style.
diagnosis: List the visual patterns used. If every section looks different, there is no repetition.
fix: Establish visual patterns early. Repeat them throughout.

name: Contrast Creates Emphasis
principle: The greater the visual contrast, the greater the emphasis.
violation: Everything is equally prominent. No element stands out.
diagnosis: Squint. What pops out? Is it the most important thing?
fix: Increase contrast for the most important element. Decrease contrast for secondary elements.

name: Emphasis Requires Restraint
principle: If everything is emphasized, nothing is.
perceptual_basis: Figure-ground. Emphasis requires a ground to stand against.
violation: Bold headers, bright colors, large text, and icons on every element simultaneously.
diagnosis: Count the elements with emphasis treatments. If more than 20%, emphasis is diluted.
fix: Limit emphasis to 1-2 elements per view. De-emphasize everything else.

name: Strategic Use of Whitespace
principle: Whitespace around an element increases its prominence by isolating it.
violation: Cramming content into every available space.
diagnosis: Add whitespace around the most important element. Does it become more prominent?
fix: Isolate key elements with generous whitespace.

name: Balance Does Not Mean Symmetry
principle: Asymmetrical balance is often more dynamic and engaging than perfect symmetry.
violation: Layout feels like it is tilting to one side.
diagnosis: Divide the page into quadrants. If one quadrant has significantly more visual weight, balance is off.
fix: Distribute visual weight. Use whitespace to counterbalance dense areas.

name: White Space Is Not Empty Space
principle: White space is an active design element, not a void to be filled.
violation: Filling every pixel with content. Viewing white space as wasted space.
diagnosis: If the page feels 'full' rather than 'complete,' white space is insufficient.
fix: Treat white space as a design element. Use it intentionally.

name: The Closure Principle
principle: The brain seeks completeness. Incomplete patterns create discomfort.
violation: Elements that feel like they are missing something. Incomplete sections.
diagnosis: Look at the bottom of the page. Does it feel finished?
fix: Ensure every section has a clear beginning, middle, and end.

name: Every Section Needs a Clear Beginning and End
principle: Transitions between sections should be intentional, not accidental.
violation: Sections that blend into each other without clear separation.
diagnosis: Can you point to exactly where one section ends and the next begins?
fix: Add clear visual separators between sections.

name: The Page Should Feel Complete at Any Stopping Point
principle: If a reader stops halfway, they should still have a coherent experience.
violation: Content that only makes sense if read in full.
diagnosis: Read only the first half of the page. Do you have a coherent understanding?
fix: Structure content so each section is self-contained.

name: The Path Should Be Obvious
principle: A reader should never wonder 'where do I look next?'
violation: Dead ends, unclear transitions, layouts that force searching.
diagnosis: Trace the path the eye would follow. Is it a smooth path or a scavenger hunt?
fix: Create clear visual pathways. Use alignment, proximity, and arrows.

name: Vertical Flow for Sequential Content
principle: Content that should be read in order needs strong vertical flow.
violation: Two-column layouts that force zigzag reading for sequential content.
diagnosis: Would a reader naturally read this top-to-bottom?
fix: Use single-column for sequential content. Two columns only for non-sequential information.

name: Scannable Structure for Reference Content
principle: Content that will be scanned needs clear scan anchors.
violation: Dense text with no headings, labels, or visual landmarks.
diagnosis: Can a reader find the information they need in under 5 seconds?
fix: Add headings, bullet points, bold key terms, visual markers.

name: Remove Before Adding
principle: Before adding any element, ask: does this serve comprehension?
violation: Adding decorative elements, redundant text, or filler content.
diagnosis: Remove one element at a time. Does comprehension improve?
fix: Remove every element that does not directly serve comprehension.

name: Simplicity Is Not Simplistic
principle: Reducing complexity is not the same as reducing depth.
violation: Either overwhelming with raw complexity or oversimplifying until meaning is lost.
diagnosis: Is the content as simple as it can be without losing meaning?
fix: Manage complexity through structure, not by removing substance.

name: Restraint in Color, Typography, and Treatment
principle: Use the minimum number of colors, fonts, and treatments needed.
violation: 5 colors, 3 fonts, and 4 decoration styles when 2 colors, 1 font, and 2 treatments would suffice.
diagnosis: List every visual variable in use. Can you reduce any without losing clarity?
fix: Reduce to the minimum variables needed for clear hierarchy.
